elements.sent-2411-001: Two-neutrino double electron capture (2$\nu$DEC) is a rare nuclear process in which two protons in a nucleus simultaneously capture two orbital electrons and are converted to neutrons.
elements.sent-2411-002: Within the framework of the Standard Model (SM) of particle physics, two neutrinos are emitted in this process.
elements.sent-2411-003:
Its inverse half-life can be expressed as  egin{equation}\label{eq:halflife_2nu} (T_{2
u})^{-1}=G_{2
u}\left|M_{2
u}ight|^2,\end{equation} where $G_{2
u}$ is the phase space factor, and $M_{2
u}$ is the nuclear matrix element (NME) for the process~\cite{Suhonen:1998ck .}
elements.sent-2411-004: If neutrinos are their own antiparticles, i.e., Majorana fermion, the process above could also have a neutrinoless mode~\cite{Avignone:2007fu , namely neutrinoless double electron capture (0$\nu$DEC).}
elements.sent-2411-005: Once this phenomenon is observed, it will confirm the Majorana nature of neutrinos, directly violate the lepton number conservation, and open new windows to physics beyond the SM~\cite{Dolinski:2019nrj, Mohapatra:1986su, Deppisch:2012nb .}
elements.sent-2411-007:
Although $M_{2
u}$ and $M_{0
u}$ are not directly related, precise measurements of the half-life of 2$
u$DEC can serve as a reference point for various NME calculation methods for both the two-neutrino and neutrino-less modes.
elements.sent-2411-008: After capturing the two electrons, the resulting two vacancies of the $^{124}$Te daughter atom are subsequently filled by emitting X-rays and/or Auger electrons, which we denote as X.
elements.sent-2411-009: The energy deposited by these emissions is on the order of 10\,keV, a range detectable by large xenon detectors such as PandaX-4T~\cite{Zhang:2018xdp , initially designed for dark matter searches.}
elements.sent-2411-010: To date, the half-life of 2$\nu$DEC in $^{124}$Xe has been measured by the XENON~\cite{XENON:2019dti,XENON:2022evz,XENONCollaboration:2022kmb and LZ collaborations~\cite{Aalbers:2024xwo .}}
elements.sent-2411-011: In this work, we report a measurement of the $^{124}$Xe 2$\nu$DEC half-life, along with the relative branching ratios for different atomic shell capture modes, based on combined data from the commissioning run (Run0) and the first science run (Run1) of the PandaX-4T experiment.
elements.sent-2411-012: Sec.~\ref{sec.Detector provides a brief overview of the PandaX-4T detector and its data-taking history.}
elements.sent-2411-013: Sec.~\ref{sec.Analysis details the data analysis, including event selection, energy reconstruction, signal and background models, and the fit procedure.}
elements.sent-2411-014: The final results and comprehensive uncertainty analysis are presented and discussed in Sec.~\ref{sec.Result .}
elements.sent-2411-015: PandaX-4T is a multi-purpose experiment located in the B2 hall of the China Jinping Underground Laboratory (CJPL-II)~\cite{Li:2014rca .}
elements.sent-2411-016: It uses natural xenon as the target material to search for dark matter signals and investigate the fundamental properties of neutrinos.
elements.sent-2411-017: The detector holds 5.6 tonnes of liquid xenon and is housed within a stainless steel water tank containing 900 tonnes of ultra-pure water for shielding against external radioactivity.
elements.sent-2411-018: The xenon cooling and purification system consists of three cooling units and two separate re-circulation loops, which continuously eliminate contaminants through hot getters~\cite{Zhao:2020vxh .}
elements.sent-2411-019: The cylindrical dual-phase time projection chamber (TPC) has a sensitive volume with 3.7 tonnes of liquid xenon in the electric field cage.
elements.sent-2411-020: A vertical electric field is established by the anode mesh, gate mesh, and cathode grid from top to bottom.
elements.sent-2411-021: The separations of neighboring electrodes are 10\,mm and 1185\,mm, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-022: The side of the electric field cage is enclosed by 24 highly reflective polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) panels, and the distance between opposite panels is 1185\,mm.
elements.sent-2411-023: The TPC is equipped with 169 and 199 Hamamatsu R11410-23 3-inch photomultiplier tubes (PMTs) at the top and bottom, respectively, which detect prompt scintillation ($S1$) and delayed electroluminescence ($S2$) photons resulting from energy depositions.
elements.sent-2411-024: These two signals are used to precisely reconstruct both the energy and the three-dimensional position of events~\cite{PandaX-4T:2021bab, PANDA-X:2021jua, PandaX:2024med .}
elements.sent-2411-025: The skin region between the TPC and the inner cryostat vessel serves as a veto system with 105 Hamamatsu R8520 1-inch PMTs instrumented on the top and bottom.
elements.sent-2411-026: The data-taking and operational history of PandaX-4T is summarized in Figure~\ref{fig:exposure_operation and is also detailed in Ref.~\cite{PandaX-4T:2021bab, PandaX:2024qfu .}}
elements.sent-2411-027: The experiment has completed two stable data-taking periods, Run0 and Run1.
elements.sent-2411-028: Run0 took place from November 28, 2020, to April 16, 2021, with 94.9 days of physics data collected.
elements.sent-2411-029: Following this, calibration and an offline distillation~\cite{Cui:2020bwf, Cui:2024ltd campaign aimed at tritium removal were conducted.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Run1 resumed data-taking from November 16, 2021, to May 15, 2022, yielding 163.6 days of physics data.
elements.sent-2411-031: Several operational and calibration campaigns, especially the xenon injection and neutron calibration, significantly impacted the background levels in this analysis and are therefore highlighted in Figure~\ref{fig:exposure_operation , with their effects discussed in detail in Sec.~\ref{subsec.bkg .}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{figures/evolution_livetime_datasets.pdf} \caption{Data taking history and accumulated live time in Run0 and Run1. Gray shaded regions indicate the periods for detector operations, and that for detector calibration after physics data taking is indicated by green shaded regions. Several important operation and calibration campaigns within Run0 are also marked, including xenon injection (yellow), AmBe calibration (magenta), and PuC calibration (red), which divides Run0 into four periods: Run0$_{a}-$Run0$_{d}$. } \label{fig:exposure_operation}
elements.sent-2411-032: The combined data from Run0 and Run1 are utilized in this analysis.
elements.sent-2411-033: The basic data processing and signal reconstruction procedures and the data selection criteria are inherited from the dark matter search analysis~\cite{PandaX:2024med .}
elements.sent-2411-034: Only the energy region of interest (ROI) and quality cuts are updated specifically for this analysis.
elements.sent-2411-035: The energy range between 25\,keV and 75\,keV is defined as the ROI in this analysis, and the specifics of energy reconstruction are provided in Sec.~\ref{subsec.energyrecon .}
elements.sent-2411-036: This range captures most of the energy peaks from the 2$\nu$DEC of $^{124}$Xe while minimizing the impact of background components such as $^{133}$Xe (see Sec.~\ref{subsec.sig and Sec.~\ref{subsubsec.activation for details).}}
elements.sent-2411-037: Unlike the possible $\mathcal{O}$(keV) signals in dark matter searches, the signals in our ROI are sufficiently large.
elements.sent-2411-038: They are not significantly affected by isolated $S1$ signals or the afterglow following high-energy events~\cite{PandaX:2023sil .}
elements.sent-2411-039: Consequently, we can recover periods excluded in Ref.~\cite{PandaX:2024qfu , and the live times for both Run0 and Run1 are equal to their calendar duration.}
elements.sent-2411-040: The same fiducial volumes (FVs) as in the dark matter search analysis are used, corresponding to fiducial masses of $2.38\pm0.04$ tonnes in Run0 and $2.48\pm0.05$ tonnes in Run1, resulting in a total exposure of 1.73 tonne$\cdot$year.
elements.sent-2411-041: Due to the low rate of non-physical noise signals within our ROI, we have relaxed the requirement of correlation between $S2$ width and drift time in Ref.~\cite{PandaX:2024med , which is caused by the electron diffusion effect during drifting.}
elements.sent-2411-042: For example, in the middle of the detector, the lower (upper) limit of the $S2$ width for 50\,keV events is relaxed from 2.5 (3.7)\,$\mu$s to 2.0 (4.2)\,$\mu$s.
elements.sent-2411-043: Additionally, the charge distribution cut and the top-bottom partition cut for the $S2$ signal have been removed, as the pulse shape of the $S2$ within the ROI is already satisfactory.
elements.sent-2411-044: By easing these cuts, we have achieved higher signal efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-045: All other cuts are consistent with those in Ref.~\cite{PandaX:2024med , with minor adjustments to extend from lower energy regions to the higher energy ROI.}
elements.sent-2411-046: The data quality criteria described above result in a stable efficiency of approximately 99.5\% for energies above 50\,keV, with a slight drop in the lower energy range, as illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:effcurve .}
elements.sent-2411-047: The efficiency is determined using $^{220}$Rn and $^{222}$Rn calibration data, and the fitted efficiency curve with associated uncertainty is then applied to the signal and background spectra in the final fit.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.7\columnwidth]{figures/eff_fit.pdf} \caption{The data quality cut efficiency derived from $^{220}$Rn calibration data (black dots). The red curve represents the exponential fit to the data, and the red shaded band represents the associated uncertainty.} \label{fig:effcurve}
elements.sent-2411-048: The energy of a given event is reconstructed as \begin{equation}E_{\rm rec} = 13.7\,{\rm eV} \times (\frac{S1}{g1} + \frac{S2_{\rm b}}{g2_{\rm b}}),\end{equation} where 13.7~eV is the average energy to generate a quanta in liquid xenon.
elements.sent-2411-049: The reconstruction parameters, ($g1$, $g2_{\rm b}$), are calibrated using the mono-energetic electron-recoil (ER) peaks including 41.5\,keV ($^{\rm 83m}$Kr), 164\,keV ($^{\rm 131m}$Xe), and 236\,keV ($^{\rm 129m}$Xe\,+\,$^{\rm 127}$Xe).
elements.sent-2411-050: Unlike the dark matter analysis in the low energy range, the values of ($g1$, $g2_{\rm b}$) are separately fitted for Run0 and Run1 using the Doke-plot method~\cite{Doke:2002oab to optimize energy reconstruction accuracy within our ROI, yielding $(0.100 \pm 0.001, 4.0 \pm 0.1)$ for Run0 and $(0.096 \pm 0.001, 4.5 \pm 0.1)$ for Run1.}
elements.sent-2411-051: The accuracy of energy reconstruction and the energy resolution are verified using the ER peaks mentioned above, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:energy .}
elements.sent-2411-052: The deviations of reconstructed energies $E_{\rm rec}$ from their theoretical values $E_{\rm true}$ are smaller than 1\% for the reconstructed energies.
elements.sent-2411-053: The energy resolution is defined by calculating the ratio of the standard deviation $\sigma$ to the expected energy $E_{\rm true}$, obtained from Gaussian fits to the reconstructed spectra.
elements.sent-2411-054: We model the energy deviation and energy resolution as functions of energy as \begin{equation}\label{eq:linearity} (E_{\rm rec}-E_{\rm true})/E_{\rm true} = a_{0}+b_{0}E_{\rm true}.\end{equation}.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=0.7\columnwidth]{figures/energy.pdf} \caption{Fractional deviation of energy reconstruction (top) and energy resolution (bottom) for three characteristic gamma peaks in Run0 (red) and Run1 (blue). The error bars reflect both statistical and systematic uncertainties. The red and blue lines represent the corresponding fits to the data.} \label{fig:energy}
elements.sent-2411-055: The signals of 2$\nu$DEC are from X-rays and/or Auger electrons.
elements.sent-2411-056: The energy carried away by the emitted neutrinos, as shown in Eq.~\ref{eq:DEC , cannot be measured.}
elements.sent-2411-057: The recoil energy of nuclei is negligible.
elements.sent-2411-058: The total energy deposited in the detector depends only on the orbitals of the two captured electrons, which can be calculated numerically.
elements.sent-2411-059: The branching ratios for capture from different sub-shells can be calculated following Ref.~\cite{Doi:1991xf, Bambynek:1977zz and be extended to all electron shells of $^{124}$Xe analogically.}
elements.sent-2411-060: The total energy deposition for the $KK$ capture mode is calculated as 64.3\,keV with a branching ratio of 72.7\%, and the energy deposition and fractions of $KL$, $KM$, $KN$, and other capture modes are also calculated and summarized in Table~\ref{tab:capture_fraction .}
elements.sent-2411-061: There are slight discrepancies between our results and the values reported in Ref.~\cite{XENON:2022evz .}
elements.sent-2411-062: The discrepancy may stem from minor differences in the double-vacancy energies and the numerical values of the outer electron wave functions used in our calculations.
elements.sent-2411-063: We attribute this discrepancy to systematic uncertainties in Sec.~\ref{sec.Result .}
elements.sent-2411-064: We consider the 2$\nu$DEC signal as four mono-energetic peaks in the ROI, ranging from the $KK$ to $KN$ transitions, with corresponding energies and fractions listed in Table~\ref{tab:capture_fraction .}
elements.sent-2411-065: The captures from subshells are merged into a single Gaussian because the branching ratios of the other levels are too small compared to the dominant term.
elements.sent-2411-066: The overall signal spectrum is represented as a weighted sum of multiple Gaussian peaks with their resolutions determined by Eq.~\ref{eq:resolution .}
elements.sent-2411-067: The energy resolutions corresponding to the $KK$ and $KL$ captures of $^{124}$Xe 2$\nu$DEC are (5.2 $\pm$ 0.1)$\%$ and (6.7 $\pm$ 0.1)$\%$, respectively.
elements.figure-2411-004: \caption{Summary of energies and capture fractions of $^{124}$Xe 2$\nu$DEC from different shell combinations. The energy range encompasses various capture combinations at different energy levels within the same energy shell. The capture fractions calculated by XENON collaboration~\cite{XENON:2022evz} are also listed here for comparison.} \begin{tabular}{c|c|c|c} Decay mode Energy (keV) Fraction (\%) Fraction in Ref.~\cite{XENON:2022evz} (\%) $KK$ 64.3 72.7 72.4 $KL$ 36.7$-$37.3 19.7 20.0 $KM$ 32.9$-$33.3 4.3 4.3 $KN$ 32.3$-$32.4 1.0 1.0 Other $\leq$ 10.0 2.3 2.3 \end{tabular} \label{tab:capture_fraction}
elements.sent-2411-068: There are two main categories of background in this analysis.
elements.sent-2411-069: Linear or continuous background spectra are extended from the dark matter search region into the ROI, including contributions from $^{214}$Pb, $^{212}$Pb, $^{85}$Kr, material radioisotopes, $^{136}$Xe, and solar neutrinos.
elements.sent-2411-070: Characteristic energy spectra specifically within the ROI are from cosmogenic and neutron-activated isotopes, including $^{127}$Xe, $^{133}$Xe, and $^{125}$I.
elements.sent-2411-071: A two-dimensional background model that incorporates energy and temporal information is employed to enhance the identification efficiency of the $^{124}$Xe 2$\nu$DEC signal.
elements.sent-2411-072: The various background types, their energy and temporal signatures, and the estimated event counts are summarized in Table~\ref{tab:bkg_pdf_count_budget and are discussed in detail below.}
elements.figure-2411-005: \caption{Summary of the background components, their spectrum and evolution signature, and corresponding expected and best-fit event counts in different data sets.} \begin{tabular}{c|c|c|c|c|c} Backgrounds Spectrum Evolution Dataset Expected Best-fit $^{214}$Pb Linear $^{222}$Rn $\alpha$ Run0 748$\pm$59 730$\pm$51 Run1 1641$\pm$130 1764$\pm$105 $^{212}$Pb Linear Constant Run0 130$\pm$35 130$\pm$32 Run1 188$\pm$50 192$\pm$47 $^{85}$Kr Linear Constant Run0 209$\pm$108 212$\pm$63 Run1 658$\pm$196 732$\pm$115 Material Linear Constant Run0 146$\pm$6 145$\pm$6 Run1 285$\pm$11 284$\pm$12 $^{136}$Xe Linear Constant Run0 245$\pm$11 243$\pm$11 Run1 437$\pm$20 437$\pm$20 Solar $\nu$ Continuous Constant Run0 85$\pm$9 84$\pm$8 Run1 153$\pm$15 153$\pm$15 $^{127}$Xe Gaussian @33\,keV Decay Run0 43$\pm$1 43$\pm$1 $^{133}$Xe Tail into 75\,keV Decay Run0$_{a}$ 0.18$\pm$0.06 0.17$\pm$0.04 Run0$_{b}$ 0.00$\pm$0.03 0.00$\pm$0.01 Run0$_{c}$ 0.4$\pm$0.1 0.4$\pm$0.1 Run0$_{d}$ 20$\pm$2 20$\pm$2 Rapid $^{125}$I Multi-Gaussian Decay Run0$_{b}$ - 5$\pm$5 Run0$_{c}$ - 7$\pm$6 Run0$_{d}$ - 54$\pm$23 Slow $^{125}$I Multi-Gaussian Decay Run0$_{a}$ - 15$\pm$7 Run0$_{b}$ - 14$\pm$6 Run0$_{c}$ - 25$\pm$12 Run0$_{d}$ - 96$\pm$27 Run1 - 40$\pm$42 \end{tabular} \label{tab:bkg_pdf_count_budget}
elements.sent-2411-073: The $\beta$ decay of $^{214}$Pb, a progeny of $^{222}$Rn, constitutes one of the most significant background contributions.
elements.sent-2411-074: There is a confirmed depletion effect~\cite{Ma:2020kll , i.e., $^{214}$Pb rate decreases with respect to the $^{222}$Rn rate due to the drifting of charged nuclei in the TPC's electric field.}
elements.sent-2411-075: The average $^{214}$Pb decay rate of $4.5 \pm 0.2\,\mu$Bq/kg in Run0 is directly measured by fitting its broad $\beta$ spectrum at high energy~\cite{PandaX:2023ggs , yielding a depletion factor of 63\%.}
elements.sent-2411-076: The same factor is applied to Run1, resulting in an average rate of $5.5 \pm 0.2\,\mu$Bq/kg based on the measured $^{222}$Rn $\alpha$ rate.
elements.sent-2411-077: The $^{214}$Pb contribution within the ROI is estimated by evaluating the ratio of events in the ROI to the full spectrum from a dedicated $^{222}$Rn calibration run and a high-precision theoretical calculation~\cite{Haselschwardt:2020iey .}
elements.sent-2411-078: The levels of $^{222}$Rn and $^{214}$Pb varied throughout both Run0 and Run1 due to online distillation and xenon circulation adjustments, and the temporal evolution of $^{214}$Pb is tracked through the measured $^{222}$Rn $\alpha$ rate.
elements.sent-2411-079: The background contribution from the $\beta$ decay of $^{212}$Pb, a daughter nucleus in the $^{220}$Rn decay chain, is estimated using procedures similar to those for $^{214}$Pb.
elements.sent-2411-080: The $^{212}$Pb activity is determined from the $^{212}$Po $\alpha$ rate and the $^{212}$Pb/$^{212}$Po ratio, which is derived from the high-energy spectrum fit in Ref.~\cite{PandaX:2023ggs .}
elements.sent-2411-081: The resulting activity is $0.30 \pm 0.08$ ($0.24 \pm 0.06$)\,$\mu$Bq/kg in Run0 (Run1).
elements.sent-2411-082: Since no temporal variations in $^{212}$Po $\alpha$ rates are observed beyond statistical fluctuations, the above values are taken as stable activities of $^{212}$Pb in Run0 and Run1.
elements.sent-2411-083: The background from $^{85}$Kr $\beta$ decay is evaluated by the correlated $\beta$-$\gamma$ emissions from the $^{85\rm m}$Rb.
elements.sent-2411-084: The concentrations of Kr in Run0 and Run1 are estimated to be $0.52 \pm 0.27$ and $0.94 \pm 0.28$ parts per trillion, respectively, assuming a $^{85}$Kr abundance of $2 \times 10^{-11}$~\cite{Collon:2004xs .}
elements.sent-2411-085: The $^{85}$Kr background rate is assumed to remain constant over both Run0 and Run1.
elements.sent-2411-086: The radioactivity in the detector materials primarily originates from $^{60}$Co, $^{40}$K, $^{232}$Th, and $^{238}$U in the PMTs and detector vessels.
elements.sent-2411-087: The radioactivity is measured with a high-purity germanium detector~\cite{PandaX-4T:2021lbm and subsequent wide energy spectrum fits, as detailed in Ref.~\cite{PandaX-4T:2022kwg .}}
elements.sent-2411-088: Their background contributions within the ROI are evaluated using the Geant4-based BambooMC simulation framework~\cite{Chen:2021asx and combined into a single background component for this analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-089: The contribution from the two-neutrino double $\beta$ decay ($2\nu\beta\beta$) of $^{136}$Xe is constrained using the half-life and isotope abundance of $^{136}$Xe measured in situ by PandaX-4T~\cite{PandaX-4T:2022kwg .}
elements.sent-2411-090: The background from solar $pp$ and $^7$Be neutrinos elastically scattering on electrons is estimated with the event rate and continuous spectrum calculated in Ref.~\cite{Chen:2016eab .}
elements.sent-2411-091: A 10\% uncertainty in the solar neutrino flux, based on the Borexino measurement~\cite{BOREXINO:2018ohr , is included in this analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-092: The background rates from material radioactivity, $^{136}$Xe $2\nu\beta\beta$, and solar neutrinos are assumed to remain unchanged in our detector, as detector operation campaigns do not influence them.
elements.sent-2411-093: Except for the continuous spectrum of the solar neutrino background, the spectra of all other backgrounds described above are generated from the BambooMC simulation and parameterized with a linear function in the ROI.
elements.sent-2411-094: During Run0, a bottle of above-ground xenon was injected into the detector, represented by the yellow shaded region in Figure~\ref{fig:exposure_operation , leading to a significant increase in cosmogenic $^{127}$Xe activity.}
elements.sent-2411-095: The decay of $^{127}$Xe via electron capture (EC) contributes to the background at 33.2\,keV, with a half-life of 36.4 days~\cite{LNHB:Xe127 .}
elements.sent-2411-096: The amount of 33.2\,keV events can be constrained by the ratio of this peak with a mono-energetic peak at 408\,keV, where 375\,keV $\gamma$-rays from the daughter $^{127}$I nucleus deposit the full energy in the TPC.
elements.sent-2411-097: The ratio is simulated with BambooMC.
elements.sent-2411-098: The 408\,keV events are also utilized to track the evolution of the $^{127}$Xe background throughout Run0.
elements.sent-2411-099: The total $^{127}$Xe background in Run0 is estimated to be $43 \pm 1$ events.
elements.sent-2411-100: The $^{127}$Xe contribution in Run1 is negligible due to the long interruption of approximately 6 months after the end of Run0.
elements.sent-2411-101: Different types of neutron sources, including $^{241}$Am-Be (AmBe) and $^{238}$Pu-C(PuC), were used in calibrations before, during, and after Run0, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:exposure_operation .}
elements.sent-2411-102: Among them, the PuC source has the strongest neutron flux.
elements.sent-2411-103: Specifically, the xenon injection, as well as the AmBe and PuC calibrations during Run0, introduced additional $^{133}$Xe and $^{125}$Xe in Run0.
elements.sent-2411-104: Therefore, Run0 is correspondingly divided into four subsets, labeled Run0$_{a}$, Run0$_{b}$, Run0$_{c}$, and Run0$_{d}$ for convenience, as illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:exposure_operation .}
elements.sent-2411-105: Their respective live times are 15.5, 14.1, 27.1, and 38.1 days.
elements.sent-2411-106: Additionally, the intensive neutron calibration conducted after Run0 may introduce residual backgrounds that persist into Run1.
elements.sent-2411-107: No neutron calibration run was conducted during the Run1 data-taking period.
elements.sent-2411-108: It undergoes $\beta$ decay to $^{133}$Cs with a half-life of 5.2\,days and transitions to the 80.1\,keV excited state with a branching ratio of 98.5\%~\cite{LNHB:Xe133 .}
elements.sent-2411-109: The $\beta$-$\gamma$ coincidence events starting at 80.1\,keV may leak into the ROI due to the energy smearing.
elements.sent-2411-110: By selecting events in the 75$-$120\,keV range for an energy-time two-dimensional side-band fit using the same background model as the final fit (See Sec.~\ref{subsec.FitMethod ), we can derive an estimation and constrain the level of $^{133}$Xe in the ROI.}
elements.sent-2411-111: The $^{133}$Xe backgrounds in four subsets, Run0$_{a -$Run0$_{d}$, are determined to be $0.18 \pm 0.06$, $0.00 \pm 0.03$, $0.4 \pm 0.1$, and $20 \pm 2$ events, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-112: The contribution of $^{133}$Xe is not considered in Run1 due to its short half-life and the relatively long interval between Run0 and Run1.
elements.sent-2411-113: The $^{125}$Xe EC decay generates 33.2\,keV depositions, most of which are associated with 243.4\,keV or 188.4\,keV $\gamma$-rays, and the estimated background within the ROI is negligible; thus it is not included in the background model.
elements.sent-2411-114: The total energy depositions for these EC processes are 67.3\,keV, 40.4\,keV, and 36.5\,keV, respectively~\cite{Deslattes:2003zz, xray:LBL .}
elements.sent-2411-115: These energy peaks closely coincide with those of the $^{124}$Xe 2$\nu$DEC, making $^{125}$I the most significant background component in this analysis.
elements.sent-2411-116: The temporal evolution of $^{125}$I inside the FV following neutron activation can be modeled as \begin{equation}N_{\rm {^{125}I}}(t) = -\kappa_1 e^{-(t-t_0)/\tau_{\rm {^{125}Xe}}} + \kappa_2 e^{-(t-t_0)/\tau_{\rm eff}} + \kappa_3 e^{-(t-t_0)/\tau_{\rm {^{125}I}}}. \label{eq:I125model}\end{equation} The first term describes the production of $^{125}$I from the decay of $^{125}$Xe according to its radioactive lifetime $\tau_{\rm {^{125 Xe }$.}}
elements.sent-2411-117: The second term accounts for both the physical decay of $^{125}$I and its continuous removal from the FV via the xenon circulation and purification system, with an effective lifetime $\tau_{\rm eff}$, which is expected to be much shorter than the physical decay lifetime $\tau_{\rm {^{125 I }$.}}
elements.sent-2411-118: Due to the stable circulation flow, the removal of $^{125}$I is consistently described by a common $\tau_{\rm eff}$ throughout Run0.
elements.sent-2411-119: The third term represents the potential contribution of $^{125}$I from the circulation-inaccessible dead zones, which diffuses into the FV and decays with its physical lifetime.
elements.sent-2411-120: A consistent phenomenon was also observed during the removal of tritium in PandaX-II~\cite{PandaX-II:2017hlx .}
elements.sent-2411-121: For clarity, we refer to this component as ``slow $^{125}$I'', and the others as ``rapid $^{125}$I''.
elements.sent-2411-122: The rapid $^{125}$I component appeared only in the subsets of Run0$_{b}$, Run0$_{c}$, and Run0$_{d}$ due to its short $\tau_{\rm eff}$.
elements.sent-2411-123: The contributions from rapid $^{125}$I in Run0$_{a}$ and Run1 are negligible since the related neutron calibration occurred several months prior to these datasets.
elements.sent-2411-124: Comparatively, the slow $^{125}$I component persists throughout the entire PandaX-4T data collection period due to its long lifetime.
elements.sent-2411-125: These contributions are float in the Run0 subsets and Run1 in the final likelihood fit.
elements.sent-2411-126: We use the unbinned maximum likelihood method to incorporate the temporal evolution of various backgrounds in the analysis.
elements.sent-2411-127: The likelihood function is constructed as \begin{equation}\label{eq:likelihood} L = \left\{\prod_{n=\rm 0,1} \left[ {\rm Poisson}(N_{obs}^n|N_{fit}^n) \times \prod_{i=1}^{N_{obs}^n} (l_{s}^{n,i}+\sum_b {l_{b}^{n,i}})\right]\right\} \times\left[\prod_{b,n}G(N_{b,fit}^{n}, N_b^{n}, \sigma_b^{n}) G(\Vec{p}, \Vec{\mu_p}, \Vec{\sigma_p})\right],\end{equation} with \begin{equation}N_{fit}^{n}=N_{s,fit}^n + \sum_bN_{b,fit}^n, \label{eq:frac_likelihood_sig} l_{s}^{n,i}=\frac{N_{s,fit}^nP_{\rm s}^n(E^i,t^i;\Vec{p})}{N_{fit}^{n}}, \label{eq:frac_likelihood_bkg} l_{b}^{n,i}=\frac{N_{b,fit}^nP_{b}^n(E^i,t^i;\Vec{p})}{N_{fit}^{n}}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-128: To distinguish between the two experimental phases, we denote the runs with the variable $n$, where $n=0$ corresponds to Run0 and $n=1$ corresponds to Run1.

u m DEC}$, the half-life is calculated with  egin{equation}\label{eq:halflife} T_{1/2}^{2
u m DEC} = {m ln\,2} 	imes  rac{N_A	imes\eta	imes \epsilon 	imes m 	imes t}{N_{2
u m DEC}	imes M_A},\end{equation} where $m 	imes t$=1.73\, ton$\cdot$yr is the total exposure, $M_A$ = 0.131\,kg/mol is the xenon molar mass, $N_A$ is the Avogadro's constant, $\eta$ = $(10.0 \pm 0.1)	imes10^{-4}$ is the isotopic abundance of $^{124}$Xe in PandaX-4T, which is measured by a residual gas analyzer, and $\epsilon=99.4\%$ is the signal efficiency of $^{124}$Xe considering both detection and quality cuts.

elements.sent-2411-138: The best-fit results, projected onto binned energy spectra and time evolution for both Run0 and Run1, are shown in Figure~\ref{fig:finalfit .}

elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{figures/fit_result.pdf} \caption{Results of the 2D unbinned likelihood fit to combined Run0 and Run1 data. For better visualization, the results are projected into binned energy spectra (top) and time evolution (bottom) for Run0 (left) and Run1 (right), respectively. The energy spectra in data are divided into 1 keV each bin, and the time evolution is unequally grouped with adjacent data, with 2$\sim$5 days each bin, for clearer display. The red solid line indicates the best-fit signal plus the background model, and the cyan solid line indicates the $^{124}$Xe 2$\nu$DEC signal. Data and fit results are shown in each figure in the top panel, and the corresponding residuals are in the bottom panel.} \label{fig:finalfit}
elements.sent-2411-142: For those backgrounds with constraints, the fitted contributions are consistent with their expected values.
elements.sent-2411-143: Notably, the temporal evolution of the $^{214}$Pb background model, represented by the dark yellow lines in Figure~\ref{fig:finalfit , aligns well with the observed temporal patterns in both Run0 and Run1 data.}
elements.sent-2411-144: The fitted $^{214}$Pb activity is in agreement with its expected value within 1\,$\sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-145: The $^{125}$I peaks around 40\,keV and 70\,keV in the energy spectrum, and the corresponding temporal evolution in Run0 data are well fitted.
elements.sent-2411-146: The effective lifetime of the rapid $^{125}$I component is fitted as (2.9 $\pm$ 2.7)\,d, which is much smaller than the physical lifetime of $^{125}$I due to the continuous purification of liquid xenon.
elements.sent-2411-147: The $^{125}$I background was present during both Run0 and Run1, with a notable increase in both the rapid and slow components following the PuC neutron calibration in late Run0 (i.e., Run0$_{d}$).
elements.sent-2411-148: The phenomenon also agrees with expectation since the PuC neutron source is much stronger than other ones.
elements.sent-2411-149: The systematic uncertainties consist of eight contributions listed in Table~\ref{tab:uncertainty .}
elements.sent-2411-150: The first four items are incorporated into the likelihood function with corresponding characteristic parameters $\Vec{p}$ constrained by Gaussian penalties.
elements.sent-2411-151: The last four items are evaluated independently.
elements.figure-2411-007: \caption{Summary of statistical and systematic uncertainties on $^{124}$Xe 2$\nu$DEC signal. } \begin{tabular}{cccc} Type Contribution Uncertainty ($10^{21}\,\rm yr$) Relative (\%) Statistical 1.45 14.1 Background estimation 0.57 5.5 Energy resolution and linearity 0.15 1.5 Selection efficiency $<0.01$ - Systematic Fiducial mass 0.06 0.6 $^{124}$Xe abundance 0.10 1.0 $^{124}$Xe 2$\nu$DEC model 0.02 0.2 Background models $<0.01$ - Fit range 0.23 2.2 Total 0.64 6.2 \end{tabular} \label{tab:uncertainty}
elements.sent-2411-152: The uncertainty on the $^{124}$Xe isotopic abundance is obtained as 1.0\% based on the PandaX-4T measurement, taking into consideration both statistical and systematic uncertainties.
elements.sent-2411-153: Our calculation of $^{124}$Xe 2$\nu$DEC modes closely agrees with that from the XENON collaboration, as discussed in Sec.~\ref{subsec.sig .}
elements.sent-2411-154: The slight difference between these two calculations introduces a minor variation of 0.2\% in the outcomes, which we account for as a systematic uncertainty.
elements.sent-2411-155: The uncertainties from the background models in time evolution are also manually estimated, particularly for $^{214}$Pb, whose event rates varied over time.
elements.sent-2411-156: Different functions, such as switching from exponential to polynomial, are applied to fit the ascending and descending slopes of $^{222}$Rn $\alpha$ decays and used as an alternative model for $^{214}$Pb evolution.
elements.sent-2411-157: However, these model variations are found to have a negligible impact on the final fit results.
elements.sent-2411-158: The systematic uncertainty from the fit range is evaluated manually by adjusting the ROI to 24$-$74\,keV and 26$-$76\,keV.
elements.sent-2411-159: These changes resulted in a small uncertainty of 2.2\% compared to the baseline fit, which also confirms the stability of the fit.
elements.sent-2411-160: The total relative systematic uncertainty is 6.2\%, calculated by summing all the systematic listed in Table~\ref{tab:uncertainty in quadrature.}
elements.sent-2411-161: The final half-life of $^{124}$Xe 2$\nu$DEC is determined to be $(1.03\pm0.15_{\rm stat}\pm0.06_{\rm sys )\times10^{22}$\,yr.}
elements.sent-2411-162: This result is consistent with calculations from the effective theory (ET) and large-scale nuclear structure model (NSM)~\cite{CoelloPerez:2018ghg within 1\,$\sigma$, and with results from the quasiparticle random-phase approximation (QRPA)~\cite{Suhonen:2013rca, Pirinen:2015sma within $\sim2\,\sigma$.}}
elements.sent-2411-163: It is also in agreement with recent experimental measurements~\cite{XENONCollaboration:2022kmb,Aalbers:2024xwo within the 1\,$\sigma$ range, as depicted in Figure~\ref{fig:nuclear_models .}}
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=0.8\columnwidth]{figures/nuclear_models.pdf} \caption{Comparison of the measured half-life with theoretical predictions~\cite{CoelloPerez:2018ghg,Suhonen:2013rca, Pirinen:2015sma} and other experiments~\cite{XENONCollaboration:2022kmb,Aalbers:2024xwo}.} \label{fig:nuclear_models}
elements.sent-2411-164: Given the many approximations inherent in the model calculations for the 2$\nu$DEC branching ratios of $^{124}$Xe, experimental measurement of these ratios remains valuable.
elements.sent-2411-165: In our analysis, we fix the relative values of the $KL/KM/KN$ branching ratios and allow the sum of these three to change along with the $KK$ branching ratio while maintaining the total sum of the four branching ratios constant.
elements.sent-2411-166: The fitted $KK$ capture branching ratio of $^{124}$Xe is $(65\pm5)\%$, in reasonable agreement with the calculated value listed in Table~\ref{tab:capture_fraction at 1.5\,$\sigma$.}
elements.sent-2411-167: In this analysis, we construct an unbinned likelihood using a time-dependent background model to measure the $^{124}$Xe 2$\nu$DEC half-life.
elements.sent-2411-168: The accuracy and resolution of energy reconstruction are optimized and quantitatively calibrated.
elements.sent-2411-169: The temporal (non)variation of various background components is carefully considered.
elements.sent-2411-170: Using a total of 1.73 tonne$\cdot$year exposure from the commissioning run and the first science run, we obtain the half-life of $^{124}$Xe 2$\nu$DEC as $(1.03\pm0.15_{\rm stat}\pm0.06_{\rm sys )\times10^{22}$\,yr.}
elements.sent-2411-171: The branching ratio of $KK$ capture is measured to be $(65\pm5)\%$.
elements.sent-2411-172: Our measurement results are in excellent agreement with the XENON and LZ values~\cite{XENONCollaboration:2022kmb,Aalbers:2024xwo .}
elements.sent-2411-173: Those measurements will be important inputs for nuclear theory models to calculate the NME of 2$\nu$DEC and 0$\nu$DEC.
elements.sent-2411-174: The precision results also demonstrate the wide application and full physics potential of large low-background liquid xenon TPCs.

u )^{-1}$, can be expressed as  egin{equation}\label{eq:halflife_2nu} (T_{2
u})^{-1}=G_{2
u}\left|M_{2
u}ight|^2,\end{equation} where $G_{2
u}$ is the phase space factor, and $M_{2
u}$ is the nuclear matrix element (NME) for the process~\cite{Suhonen:1998ck .}}
elements.sent-2411-180: If neutrinos are their own antiparticles, i.e., Majorana fermions, the process above could also have a neutrinoless mode~\cite{Avignone:2007fu , namely neutrinoless double electron capture (0$\nu$DEC).}

Although $M_{2
u}$ and $M_{0
u}$ are not directly related, precise measurements of the half-life of 2$

elements.sent-2411-183: Besides $^{124}$Xe, other isotopes that have been observed to undergo 2$\nu$DEC include $^{78}$Kr~\cite{Gavrilyuk:2013yqa,Ratkevich:2017kaz and $^{130}$Ba~\cite{PUJOL20096834,Meshik:2001ra .}}
elements.sent-2411-184: In this work, we report a measurement of the $^{124}$Xe 2$\nu$DEC half-life, along with the relative capture fractions for different atomic shell capture modes, based on combined data from the commissioning run (Run0) and the first science run (Run1) of the PandaX-4T experiment.
elements.sent-2411-185: Following this, calibration and an offline distillation campaign aimed at tritium removal were conducted~\cite{Cui:2020bwf, Cui:2024ltd .}
elements.figure-2411-009: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{figures/evolution_livetime_datasets.pdf} \caption{Data taking history and accumulated live time in Run0 and Run1. Gray shaded regions indicate the periods for hardware tests, and that for detector calibration after physics data taking is indicated by green shaded regions. Several important operation and calibration campaigns within Run0 are also marked, including xenon injection (yellow), AmBe calibration (magenta), and PuC calibration (red), which divides Run0 into four periods: Run0$_{a}-$Run0$_{d}$. } \label{fig:exposure_operation}
elements.subsection-2411-006: Data selection and data quality cuts
elements.sent-2411-186: Only the energy region of interest (ROI) and data quality cuts are updated specifically for this analysis.
elements.sent-2411-187: Bottom-only S2 is used for energy reconstruction to avoid the effect of dead channels and saturation of the top PMTs.
elements.sent-2411-188: The energy of a given event is reconstructed as \begin{equation}E_{\rm rec} = W \times (\frac{Q^{c}_{\rm S1}}{g1} + \frac{Q^{c}_{\rm S2_{\rm b}}}{g2_{\rm b}}),\end{equation} where $W$ = 13.7~eV is the average energy to generate quanta in liquid xenon ~\cite{Szydagis:2011tk .}
